passages.title: Alexander Henderson (1831-1913)
passages.text:
Alexander Henderson was born in Scotland in 1831 and was the son of a successful merchant. His grandfather had founded the family business and later became the first chairman of the National Bank of Scotland. The family owned Press Estate, some 650 acres of farmland about 35 miles southeast of Edinburgh, and Alexander spent much of his childhood in the area, playing on the beach near Eyemouth or fishing in the nearby streams.

He was educated at Murcheston Academy on the outskirts of Edinburgh, returning to Press at weekends. In 1849 he began a three-year apprenticeship to become an accountant. Although he never liked the prospect of a business career, he stayed with it to please his family. In October 1855, he emigrated to Canada with his wife Agnes Elder Robertson and they settled in Montreal.

Henderson learned photography in Montreal around 1857 and quickly became a personal friend and colleague of the noted Scottish-Canadian photographer William Notman. The two men made a photographic excursion to Niagara Falls in 1860 and cooperated on experiments with magnesium flares as a source of artificial light in 1865. They were also founding members of the Art Association of Montreal, and Henderson chaired its first meeting in Notman's studio on 11 January 1860.

Despite their friendship, their styles of photography were quite different. While Notman's landscapes were noted for their bold realism, Henderson for the first 20 years of his career produced romantic images, showing the strong influence of the British landscape tradition. In 1865 he published his first major collection of landscape photographs, Canadian Views and Studies, of which only seven copies have ever been found.

In 1866, he gave up his business to open a photographic studio, advertising himself as a portrait and landscape photographer. From about 1870 he dropped portraiture to specialise in landscape photography. His numerous photographs of city life, street scenes, houses and markets are alive with human activity, and although his favourite subject was landscape he usually composed his scenes around human pursuits such as farming, cutting ice on the river, or boating. Before the late 1880s, there was little hobby or amateur competition in photography due to the time-consuming techniques involved and the weight of the equipment. People wanted to buy photographs as souvenirs of a trip or as gifts, and Henderson kept a large stock of photographs for this purpose, in formats including prints, cabinets, and albums.

Henderson exhibited his work in Montreal, London, Edinburgh, Dublin, Paris, New York, and Philadelphia. He won first prizes at exhibitions in New York in 1877 and 1878, and second prize at the world exhibition in Paris in 1878.

In the 1870s and 1880s he travelled widely throughout Quebec and Ontario, documenting the landscape and the people. He was especially fond of the wilderness and often travelled by canoe on the Blanche, du Lievre, and other eastern rivers. In 1872 he was commissioned to photograph the construction of the Intercolonial Railway, and this led in 1875 to a further commission to record the principal structures along the almost-completed line connecting Montreal to Halifax. In 1885 he went west along the Canadian Pacific Railway (CPR) to Rogers Pass in British Columbia, where he photographed the mountains and the progress of construction.

In 1892 Henderson accepted a full-time position with the CPR as manager of a photographic department. He continued in this post until 1897, when he retired completely from photography.

When Henderson died in 1913, his huge collection of glass negatives was stored in the basement of his house. Today collections of his work are held at the National Archives of Canada, Ottawa, and the McCord Museum of Canadian History, Montreal.

questions.question: Henderson rarely visited the area around Press estate when he was younger.
questions.explanation: EN: He spent much of his childhood there, playing on the beach and fishing. | CN: 他童年大部分时间都在那里度过，在海滩玩耍和钓鱼。

questions.question: Henderson pursued a business career because it was what his family wanted.
questions.explanation: EN: Although he never liked the prospect, he stayed with it to please his family. | CN: 虽然他不喜欢，但为了让家人高兴而坚持了下来。

questions.question: Henderson and Notman were surprised by the results of their 1865 experiment.
questions.explanation: EN: The experiment with magnesium flares is mentioned but there is no mention of surprise at the results. | CN: 提到了镁光实验，但未提及对结果的惊讶。

questions.question: There were many similarities between Henderson's early landscapes and those of Notman.
questions.explanation: EN: Their styles of photography were quite different - Notman's bold realism vs Henderson's romantic images. | CN: 他们的摄影风格大不相同。

questions.question: The studio that Henderson opened in 1866 was close to his home.
questions.explanation: EN: The passage mentions he opened a studio in 1866 but does not state its location relative to his home. | CN: 文章提到他1866年开了工作室，但未提及其与家的距离。

questions.question: Henderson gave up portraiture so that he could focus on taking photographs of scenery.
questions.explanation: EN: From about 1870 he dropped portraiture to specialise in landscape photography. | CN: 大约从1870年起他放弃人像摄影，专门从事风景摄影。

questions.question: When Henderson began work for the Intercolonial Railway, the Montreal to Halifax line had been finished.
questions.explanation: EN: He was commissioned to record structures along the almost-completed line. | CN: 他被委托记录几乎完工的铁路沿线结构。

questions.question: Henderson's last work as a photographer was with the Canadian Pacific Railway.
questions.explanation: EN: He worked for CPR until 1897 when he retired completely from photography. | CN: 他在CPR工作到1897年，之后完全退休不再摄影。

questions.question: Henderson's father was a ______.
questions.explanation: EN: Alexander Henderson was born in Scotland in 1831 and was the son of a successful merchant. | CN: Alexander Henderson 1831年出生于苏格兰，是一位成功商人的儿子。

questions.question: People bought photos rather than take them because photography took time and the ______ was heavy.
questions.explanation: EN: Before the late 1880s, there was little amateur competition due to time-consuming techniques and the weight of the equipment. | CN: 1880年代后期前，由于技术耗时且设备沉重，业余竞争者很少。

questions.question: The photos Henderson sold were souvenirs or ______.
questions.explanation: EN: People wanted to buy photographs as souvenirs of a trip or as gifts. | CN: 人们想购买照片作为旅行纪念品或礼物。

questions.question: He took many trips along eastern rivers in a ______.
questions.explanation: EN: He was especially fond of the wilderness and often travelled by canoe. | CN: 他特别喜欢荒野，经常乘独木舟旅行。

questions.question: In 1885 for the CPR, he photographed the ______ and the railway at Rogers Pass.
questions.explanation: EN: He went west to Rogers Pass where he photographed the mountains and the progress of construction. | CN: 他向西前往Rogers Pass，拍摄了山脉和建设进展。

passages.title: A second attempt at domesticating the tomato
passages.text:
It took at least 3,000 years for humans to learn how to domesticate the wild tomato and cultivate it for food. Now two separate teams in Brazil and China have done it all over again in less than three years. And they have done it better in some ways, as the re-domesticated tomatoes are more nutritious than the ones we eat at present.

This approach relies on the revolutionary CRISPR genome editing technique, in which changes are deliberately made to the DNA of a living cell, allowing genetic material to be added, removed or altered. The technique could not only improve existing crops, but could also be used to turn thousands of wild plants into useful and appealing foods. In fact, a third team in the US has already begun to do this with a relative of the tomato called the groundcherry.

This fast-track domestication could help make the world's food supply healthier and far more resistant to diseases, such as the rust fungus devastating wheat crops.

'This could transform what we eat,' says Jorg Kudla at the University of Munster in Germany, a member of the Brazilian team. 'There are 50,000 edible plants in the world, but 90 percent of our energy comes from just 15 crops.'

'We can now mimic the known domestication course of major crops like rice, maize, sorghum or others,' says Caixia Gao of the Chinese Academy of Sciences in Beijing. 'Then we might try to domesticate plants that have never been domesticated.'

Wild tomatoes, which are native to the Andes region in South America, produce pea-sized fruits. Over many generations, peoples such as the Aztecs and Incas transformed the plant by selecting and breeding plants with mutations in their genetic structure, which resulted in desirable traits such as larger fruit.

But every time a single plant with a mutation is taken from a larger population for breeding, much genetic diversity is lost. And sometimes the desirable mutations come with less desirable traits. For instance, the tomato strains grown for supermarkets have lost much of their flavour.

By comparing the genomes of modern plants to those of their wild relatives, biologists have been working out what genetic changes occurred as plants were domesticated. The teams in Brazil and China have now used this knowledge to reintroduce these changes from scratch while maintaining or even enhancing the desirable traits of wild strains.

Kudla's team made six changes altogether. For instance, they tripled the size of fruit by editing a gene called FRUIT WEIGHT, and increased the number of tomatoes per truss by editing another called MULTIFLORA.

While the historical domestication of tomatoes reduced levels of the red pigment lycopene - thought to have potential health benefits - the team in Brazil managed to boost it instead. The wild tomato has twice as much lycopene as cultivated ones; the newly domesticated one has five times as much.

'They are quite tasty,' says Kudla. 'A little bit strong. And very aromatic.'

The team in China re-domesticated several strains of wild tomatoes with desirable traits lost in domesticated tomatoes. In this way they managed to create a strain resistant to a common disease called bacterial spot race, which can devastate yields. They also created another strain that is more salt tolerant - and has higher levels of vitamin C.

Meanwhile, Joyce Van Eck at the Boyce Thompson Institute in New York state decided to use the same approach to domesticate the groundcherry or goldenberry (Physalis pruinosa) for the first time. This fruit looks similar to the closely related Cape gooseberry (Physalis peruviana).

Groundcherries are already sold to a limited extent in the US but they are hard to produce because the plant has a sprawling growth habit and the small fruits fall off the branches when ripe. Van Eck's team has edited the plants to increase fruit size, make their growth more compact and to stop fruits dropping. 'There's potential for this to be a commercial crop,' says Van Eck. But she adds that taking the work further would be expensive because of the need to pay for a licence for the CRISPR technology and get regulatory approval.

This approach could boost the use of many obscure plants, says Jonathan Jones of the Sainsbury Lab in the UK. But it will be hard for new foods to grow so popular with farmers and consumers that they become new staple crops, he thinks.

The three teams already have their eye on other plants that could be 'catapulted into the mainstream', including foxtail, oat-grass and cowpea. By choosing wild plants that are drought or heat tolerant, says Gao, we could create crops that will thrive even as the planet warms.

But Kudla didn't want to reveal which species were in his team's sights, because CRISPR has made the process so easy. 'Any one with the right skills could go to their lab and do this.'

questions.question: Which paragraph contains a reference to a type of tomato that can resist a dangerous infection?
questions.explanation: EN: Paragraph C mentions a strain resistant to a common disease called bacterial spot race. | CN: C段提到了一个能抵抗常见病害细菌性斑点病的品种。

questions.question: Which paragraph explains how problems can arise from focusing only on a certain type of tomato plant?
questions.explanation: EN: Paragraph B explains problems from selecting mutations: loss of genetic diversity and less desirable traits. | CN: B段解释了从突变选择的问题：遗传多样性丧失和不良性状。

questions.question: Which paragraph gives examples of plants that are not cultivated at present but could be useful as food sources?
questions.explanation: EN: Paragraph E lists foxtail, oat-grass and cowpea as plants that could be catapulted into the mainstream. | CN: E段列举了狐尾粟、燕麦草和豇豆作为可被推入主流的植物。

questions.question: Which paragraph makes a comparison between the early domestication of the tomato and more recent research?
questions.explanation: EN: Paragraph A compares the original 3,000-year domestication to new research done in under 3 years. | CN: A段将原本3000年的驯化与新研究不到3年完成进行对比。

questions.question: Which paragraph contains a personal reaction to the flavour of a tomato that has been genetically edited?
questions.explanation: EN: Paragraph C has Kudla saying They are quite tasty, a little bit strong, and very aromatic. | CN: C段中Kudla说它们很好吃，有点浓烈，非常芳香。

questions.question: Which researcher says domestication of certain plants could allow them to adapt to future environmental challenges?
questions.explanation: EN: Gao says choosing drought/heat-tolerant wild plants could create crops that thrive even as the planet warms. | CN: Gao表示选择耐旱耐热的野生植物可创造在全球变暖下仍能茁壮成长的作物。

questions.question: Which researcher thinks the idea of growing and eating unusual plants may not be accepted on a large scale?
questions.explanation: EN: Jones says it will be hard for new foods to grow so popular that they become new staple crops. | CN: Jones表示新食物很难变得如此受欢迎以至于成为新的主粮作物。

questions.question: Which researcher says it is not advisable for the future direction of certain research to be made public?
questions.explanation: EN: Kudla didn't want to reveal which species were in his team's sights. | CN: Kudla不想透露他的团队瞄准了哪些物种。

questions.question: Which researcher notes that present efforts to domesticate one wild fruit are limited by the costs involved?
questions.explanation: EN: Van Eck says taking the work further would be expensive due to licence fees and regulatory approval. | CN: Van Eck表示进一步推进工作需要昂贵的许可费和监管审批。

questions.question: Which researcher points out that humans only make use of a small proportion of the plant food available on Earth?
questions.explanation: EN: Kudla says there are 50,000 edible plants but 90 percent of our energy comes from just 15 crops. | CN: Kudla表示有5万种可食用植物，但90%的能量仅来自15种作物。

questions.question: An undesirable trait such as loss of ______ may be caused by a mutation in a tomato gene.
questions.explanation: EN: The tomato strains grown for supermarkets have lost much of their flavour. | CN: 为超市种植的番茄品种失去了大部分风味。

questions.question: By modifying one gene in a tomato plant, researchers made the tomato three times its original ______.
questions.explanation: EN: They tripled the size of fruit by editing a gene called FRUIT WEIGHT. | CN: 他们通过编辑FRUIT WEIGHT基因将果实大小增加了三倍。

questions.question: A type of tomato which was not badly affected by ______, and was rich in vitamin C, was produced in China.
questions.explanation: EN: The team in China created a strain that is more salt tolerant and has higher levels of vitamin C. | CN: 中国团队创造了一个更耐盐且维生素C含量更高的品种。

passages.title: What is exploration?
passages.text:
We are all explorers. Our desire to discover, and then share that new-found knowledge, is part of what makes us human - indeed, this has played an important part in our success as a species. Long before the first caveman slumped down beside the fire and grunted news that there were plenty of wildebeest over yonder, our ancestors had learnt the value of sending out scouts to investigate the unknown. This questing nature of ours undoubtedly helped our species spread around the globe, just as it nowadays no doubt helps the last nomadic Penan maintain their existence in the depleted forests of Borneo, and a visitor negotiate the subways of New York.

Over the years, we've come to think of explorers as a peculiar breed - different from the rest of us, different from those of us who are merely 'well travelled', even; and perhaps there is a type of person more suited to seeking out the new, a type of caveman more inclined to risk venturing out. That, however, doesn't take away from the fact that we all have this enquiring instinct, even today; and that in all sorts of professions - whether artist, marine biologist or astronomer - borders of the unknown are being tested each day.

Thomas Hardy set some of his novels in Egdon Heath, a fictional area of uncultivated land, and used the landscape to suggest the desires and fears of his characters. He is delving into matters we all recognise because they are common to humanity. This is surely an act of exploration, and into a world as remote as the author chooses. Explorer and travel writer Peter Fleming talks of the moment when the explorer returns to the existence he has left behind with his loved ones. The traveller who has for weeks or months seen himself only as a puny and irrelevant alien crawling laboriously over a country in which he has no roots and no background, suddenly encounters his other self, a relatively solid figure, with a place in the minds of certain people.

In this book about the exploration of the earth's surface, I have confined myself to those whose travels were real and who also aimed at more than personal discovery. But that still left me with another problem: the word 'explorer' has become associated with a past era. We think back to a golden age, as if exploration peaked somehow in the 19th century - as if the process of discovery is now on the decline, though the truth is that we have named only one and a half million of this planet's species, and there may be more than 10 million - and that's not including bacteria. We have studied only 5 per cent of the species we know. We have scarcely mapped the ocean floors, and know even less about ourselves; we fully understand the workings of only 10 per cent of our brains.

Here is how some of today's 'explorers' define the word. Ran Fiennes, dubbed the 'greatest living explorer', said, 'An explorer is someone who has done something that no human has done before - and also done something scientifically useful.' Chris Bonington, a leading mountaineer, felt exploration was to be found in the act of physically touching the unknown: 'You have to have gone somewhere new.' Robin Hanbury-Tenison, a campaigner on behalf of remote so-called 'tribal' peoples, said, 'A traveller simply records information about some far-off world, and reports back; but an explorer changes the world.' Wilfred Thesiger, who crossed Arabia's Empty Quarter in 1946, and belongs to an era of unmechanised travel now lost to the rest of us, told me, 'If I'd gone across by camel when I could have gone by car, it would have been a stunt.' To him, exploration meant bringing back information from a remote place regardless of any great self-discovery.

Each definition is slightly different - and tends to reflect the field of endeavour of each pioneer. It was the same whoever I asked: the prominent historian would say exploration was a thing of the past, the cutting-edge scientist would say it was of the present. And so on. They each set their own particular criteria; the common factor in their approach being that they all had, unlike many of us who simply enjoy travel or discovering new things, both a very definite objective from the outset and also a desire to record their findings.

I'd best declare my own bias. As a writer, I'm interested in the exploration of ideas. I've done a great many expeditions and each one was unique. I've lived for months alone with isolated groups of people all around the world, even two uncontacted tribes. But none of these things is of the slightest interest to anyone unless, through my books, I've found a new slant, explored a new idea. Why? Because the world has moved on. The time has long passed for the great continental voyages - another walk to the poles, another crossing of the Empty Quarter. We know how the land surface of our planet lies; exploration of it is now down to the details - the habits of microbes, say, or the grazing behaviour of buffalo. Aside from the deep sea and deep underground, it's the era of specialists. However, this is to disregard the role the human mind has in conveying remote places; and this is what interests me: how a fresh interpretation, even of a well-travelled route, can give its readers new insights.

questions.question: Why does the writer reference the New York subway in the first paragraph?
options: A. exploration is an intrinsic element of being human
B. modern city life requires exploration skills
C. New York is particularly difficult to navigate
D. subways represent the modern equivalent of wilderness
questions.explanation: EN: The New York subway example illustrates how our questing nature is part of what makes us human. | CN: 纽约地铁的例子说明了探索本能是人性的一部分。

questions.question: What does the writer suggest about explorers in the second paragraph?
options: A. Their experiences are different from those of other people
B. They are more suited to physical challenges
C. They act on an urge that is common to everyone
D. They tend to be more creative than most people
questions.explanation: EN: The writer says we all have this enquiring instinct, even today. | CN: 作者说我们所有人都有这种探索本能，即使在今天。

questions.question: What does the writer suggest about Thomas Hardy in the third paragraph?
options: A. He wrote about landscapes he had visited
B. His exploration of real places inspired his writing
C. His aim was to investigate people's emotional states
D. His novels were based on his own travel experiences
questions.explanation: EN: Hardy used the landscape to suggest the desires and fears of his characters; he delved into matters common to humanity. | CN: Hardy用风景来暗示人物的欲望和恐惧；他深入研究人类共通的事物。

questions.question: In the fourth paragraph, the writer suggests that we are wrong to think that:
options: A. the golden age of exploration existed
B. exploration is only about physical travel
C. modern technology has made exploration easier
D. exploration is no longer necessary
questions.explanation: EN: The golden age idea is presented as wrong; we have barely scratched the surface of discovery. | CN: 黄金时代的想法被呈现为错误的；我们仅仅触及了发现的表面。

questions.question: What common theme does the writer identify in the various definitions of exploration?
options: A. people tend to relate exploration to their own professional interests
B. exploration is always about going somewhere new
C. the best explorers combine physical and intellectual challenges
D. exploration must result in a published record
questions.explanation: EN: Each definition tends to reflect the field of endeavour of each pioneer. | CN: 每个定义往往反映了各先驱者的专业领域。

questions.question: What does the writer consider most important about exploration in the final paragraph?
options: A. visiting places that few others have been to
B. the human ability to cast new light on places that may be familiar
C. documenting discoveries for future generations
D. the physical challenge of reaching remote locations
questions.explanation: EN: The writer is interested in how a fresh interpretation, even of a well-travelled route, can give its readers new insights. | CN: 作者感兴趣的是新鲜诠释如何为读者带来新见解，即使是对一条常走的路线。

questions.question: Which explorer described the feelings on coming back home after a long journey?
questions.explanation: EN: Peter Fleming talks of the moment when the explorer returns to the existence he has left behind. | CN: Peter Fleming谈到了探险者回归旧日生活的时刻。

questions.question: Which explorer said exploration must involve doing something that has never been done before, with scientific value?
questions.explanation: EN: Ran Fiennes said an explorer is someone who has done something no human has done before and also done something scientifically useful. | CN: Ran Fiennes表示探险者是做了前人未做之事且有科学价值之事的人。

questions.question: Which explorer worked for the benefit of specific groups of people?
questions.explanation: EN: Robin Hanbury-Tenison was a campaigner on behalf of remote so-called tribal peoples. | CN: Robin Hanbury-Tenison是为偏远部落人民奔走的倡导者。

questions.question: Which explorer did not consider learning about oneself to be an essential part of exploration?
questions.explanation: EN: To Wilfred Thesiger, exploration meant bringing back information regardless of any great self-discovery. | CN: 对Wilfred Thesiger而言，探索意味着带回信息，而非任何伟大的自我发现。

questions.question: Which explorer referred to the relevance of the form of transport used?
questions.explanation: EN: Thesiger said If I'd gone across by camel when I could have gone by car, it would have been a stunt. | CN: Thesiger说如果我可以开车却选择骑骆驼穿越，那就会成为一种噱头。

questions.question: The writer has experience of a large number of unique ______.
questions.explanation: EN: I've done a great many expeditions and each one was unique. | CN: 我进行了大量的探险，每一次都是独一无二的。

questions.question: The writer lived with previously ______ tribes.
questions.explanation: EN: I've lived for months alone with isolated groups, even two uncontacted tribes. | CN: 我曾独自与世隔绝的群体一起生活数月，甚至是两个未接触过的部落。

questions.question: We know how the ______ of our planet lies, so exploration is now down to the details.
questions.explanation: EN: We know how the land surface of our planet lies; exploration of it is now down to the details. | CN: 我们知道地球的陆地表面是怎样的，现在的探索已进入细节层面。
